nodes.description: Altered cerebello-thalamic pathway function is the central motor-network event linking upstream SGCE mechanisms to myoclonus and dystonia.

evidence.reference_title: Myoclonus-dystonia: classification, phenomenology, pathogenesis, and treatment.
evidence.snippet: Accumulating evidence suggests that an alteration in cerebello-thalamic pathway function may play a prominent role and that this is possibly related to a GABAergic deficit reflecting Purkinje cell dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Twenty years on: Myoclonus-dystonia and ε-sarcoglycan - neurodevelopment, channel, and signaling dysfunction.
evidence.snippet: Finally, we critically discuss the normal and pathological function of the epsilon-sarcoglycan gene and its product, suggesting a role in the stabilization of the dopaminergic membrane via regulation of calcium homeostasis and in the neurodevelopmental process involving the cerebello-thalamo-pallido-cortical network.

nodes.description: Anticholinergic pharmacotherapy may improve dystonia in myoclonus-dystonia syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Anticholinergic medication may improve dystonia.

nodes.description: Anxiety disorders are common non-motor manifestations in SGCE-related myoclonus-dystonia.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000739 | Anxiety | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: SGCE mutations cause psychiatric disorders: clinical and genetic characterization.
evidence.snippet: Generalized anxiety disorder (P = 0.003) and alcohol dependence (P = 0.02) were five times more likely in mutation positive cases than tremor controls.

nodes.description: Botulinum toxin injection is a focal treatment option for cervical dystonia in myoclonus-dystonia syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Botulinum toxin injection may be especially helpful for cervical dystonia.

nodes.description: Epsilon-sarcoglycan dysfunction may alter calcium homeostasis and dopaminergic membrane stabilization, contributing to abnormal cerebello-thalamo-pallido-cortical network development and function.

evidence.reference_title: Twenty years on: Myoclonus-dystonia and ε-sarcoglycan - neurodevelopment, channel, and signaling dysfunction.
evidence.snippet: Finally, we critically discuss the normal and pathological function of the epsilon-sarcoglycan gene and its product, suggesting a role in the stabilization of the dopaminergic membrane via regulation of calcium homeostasis and in the neurodevelopmental process involving the cerebello-thalamo-pallido-cortical network.

nodes.description: Cervical dystonia or torticollis is a common focal dystonia manifestation.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000473 | Torticollis | Frequent (79-30%)
Approximately 50% of affected individuals have additional focal or segmental dystonia, presenting as cervical dystonia and/or writer's cramp.

nodes.description: Benzodiazepines, particularly clonazepam, may improve myoclonus symptoms in myoclonus-dystonia syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Benzodiazepines (particularly clonazepam) and anti-seizure drugs used to treat myoclonus (especially valproate and levitiracetam) also improve myoclonus in individuals with myoclonus-dystonia.

nodes.description: Obsessive-compulsive symptoms and compulsivity are frequent non-motor manifestations.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000722 | Compulsive behaviors | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: SGCE mutations cause psychiatric disorders: clinical and genetic characterization.
evidence.snippet: Obsessive-compulsive disorder was eight times more likely (P < 0.001) in mutation positive cases, compulsivity being the predominant feature (P < 0.001).

nodes.description: Pallidal deep brain stimulation is a refractory treatment option for medically refractory myoclonus-dystonia, with long-term evidence favoring GPi stimulation for sustained motor and disability improvement.

evidence.reference_title: Long-term effects of pallidal and thalamic deep brain stimulation in myoclonus dystonia.
evidence.snippet: We present a cohort of MD patients with very long follow-up of pallidal and/or thalamic DBS that supports the GPi as the favourable stimulation target in MD with safe and sustaining effects on motor symptoms (myoclonus>dystonia) and disability.

nodes.description: Depression is included among frequent psychiatric manifestations in the Orphanet phenotype profile.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000716 | Depression | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Disturbed serotonin homeostasis is implicated as another neurotransmitter mechanism in the myoclonus-dystonia motor circuit.

evidence.reference_title: Myoclonus-dystonia: classification, phenomenology, pathogenesis, and treatment.
evidence.snippet: Impaired striatal plasticity and disturbed serotonin homeostasis may also be implicated.

nodes.description: Dystonia accompanies myoclonus and may be focal or segmental.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001332 | Dystonia | Very frequent (99-80%)
Approximately 50% of affected individuals have additional focal or segmental dystonia, presenting as cervical dystonia and/or writer's cramp.

nodes.description: SGCE pathogenic variants reduce functional epsilon-sarcoglycan at the plasma membrane through intracellular retention, polyubiquitination, and proteasomal degradation of mutant protein.

evidence.reference_title: SGCE missense mutations that cause myoclonus-dystonia syndrome impair epsilon-sarcoglycan trafficking to the plasma membrane: modulation by ubiquitination and torsinA.
evidence.snippet: In contrast to the wild-type protein, disease-associated epsilon-sarcoglycan missense mutations (H36P, H36R and L172R) produce proteins that are undetectable at the cell surface and are retained intracellularly.

evidence.reference_title: SGCE missense mutations that cause myoclonus-dystonia syndrome impair epsilon-sarcoglycan trafficking to the plasma membrane: modulation by ubiquitination and torsinA.
evidence.snippet: These mutant proteins become polyubiquitinated and are rapidly degraded by the proteasome.

nodes.description: Myoclonus-dystonia motor-network dysfunction may involve a GABAergic deficit reflecting Purkinje-cell dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Myoclonus-dystonia: classification, phenomenology, pathogenesis, and treatment.
evidence.snippet: Accumulating evidence suggests that an alteration in cerebello-thalamic pathway function may play a prominent role and that this is possibly related to a GABAergic deficit reflecting Purkinje cell dysfunction.

nodes.description: Impaired striatal plasticity is implicated as a basal-ganglia circuit mechanism contributing to the myoclonus-dystonia motor phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Myoclonus-dystonia: classification, phenomenology, pathogenesis, and treatment.
evidence.snippet: Impaired striatal plasticity and disturbed serotonin homeostasis may also be implicated.

nodes.description: Upper-limb and other limb myoclonic jerks are characteristic, supporting the prominent upper-body phenotype.

evidence.snippet: HP:0045084 | Limb myoclonus | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Rapid brief myoclonic jerks are a defining and often predominant motor manifestation of myoclonus-dystonia syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001336 | Myoclonus | Very frequent (99-80%)
The myoclonic jerks typical of SGCE-M-D most often affect the neck, trunk, and upper limbs with less common involvement of the legs.

nodes.description: Panic attacks are included among frequent psychiatric manifestations in the Orphanet phenotype profile.

evidence.snippet: HP:0025269 | Panic attack | Frequent (79-30%)
SGCE | sarcoglycan epsilon | hgnc:10808 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in the gene encoding epsilon-sarcoglycan cause myoclonus-dystonia syndrome.
evidence.snippet: SGCE is expressed in all brain regions examined. Pedigree analysis shows a marked difference in penetrance depending on the parental origin of the disease allele.

nodes.description: SGCE pathogenic variants have pleiotropic neuropsychiatric effects that increase compulsivity, anxiety, alcohol dependence, and other psychiatric symptoms in addition to the motor phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: SGCE mutations cause psychiatric disorders: clinical and genetic characterization.
evidence.snippet: SGCE mutations are associated with a specific psychiatric phenotype consisting of compulsivity, anxiety and alcoholism in addition to the characteristic motor phenotype.

nodes.description: Orphanet lists spinal myoclonus as a very frequent myoclonus subtype in the syndrome phenotype profile.

evidence.snippet: HP:0010531 | Spinal myoclonus | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Writer's cramp is a frequent focal hand dystonia presentation.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002356 | Writer's cramp | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Zonisamide has randomized controlled trial evidence for improving myoclonus, myoclonus-related disability, and dystonia in myoclonus-dystonia syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: A randomized, controlled, double-blind, crossover trial of zonisamide in myoclonus-dystonia.
evidence.snippet: CLASSIFICATION OF EVIDENCE: This study provides Class I evidence that zonisamide improves myoclonus and related disability in patients with myoclonus-dystonia.

evidence.reference_title: A randomized, controlled, double-blind, crossover trial of zonisamide in myoclonus-dystonia.
evidence.snippet: Zonisamide also significantly improved dystonia (BFM movement) compared to placebo
